concerns.text: The plan proposes a 'Held-Out Ecosystem' validation where the model is trained on N-1 ecosystems and tested on the Nth. However, the data model (data-model.md) and preprocessing logic (Phase 1) construct the feature matrix at the ecosystem level (rows: pairs within an ecosystem). If the model is trained on N-1 ecosystems, the held-out ecosystem's species (and thus its trait vectors) are entirely unseen. Standard Random Forest cannot generalize to unseen categorical levels (e.g., new species IDs) or unseen trait distributions without explicit encoding strategies (e.g., species-agnostic embeddings or trait-only features). The plan does not specify how the model handles the 'unseen species' problem in the held-out set, rendering the generalization test invalid as currently designed.

The research doc acknowledges N ~ 8-10 ecosystems is low for meta-analysis but proceeds with a 'Held-Out Ecosystem' validation. With only ~8-10 data points (ecosystems), a leave-one-out or N-1 split results in a test set of size 1. A single data point cannot provide a statistically robust estimate of generalization error or support a p-value calculation against a null model for that specific ecosystem. The plan conflates 'internal cross-validation' (which has sufficient samples) with 'external generalization' (which has N=1). This design cannot support the claim of 'Ecological Generalizability' (Constitution Principle VI) with the proposed sample size.

Phase 1 generates negative samples as 'co-occurring unobserved pairs'. The plan assumes spatial/temporal co-occurrence data is sufficient to define the 'negative' space. However, in ecological networks, 'absence of evidence is not evidence of absence'. Many unobserved pairs are simply unsampled, not true non-interactions. Treating all unobserved co-occurring pairs as '0' (true negatives) introduces massive label noise (false negatives). This label noise will severely bias the Random Forest's decision boundary and inflate the 'trait gap' (SC-004), as the model will learn to predict 'no interaction' for many true interactions that were merely missed in the survey. The plan lacks a strategy to account for this observation bias (e.g., occupancy modeling or sampling effort covariates).
